museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: April is ports, delayed projects and late-month PlayStation weight.
museum.curatorNote: Verified and edited. April complete my friends.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: April 2026 was a month of long-awaited arrivals. After years of delays, trailers and speculation, Pragmata finally reached players, while Bethesda's Starfield made its debut on PlayStation 5, continuing the industry's shift away from traditional platform boundaries. Nintendo's calendar remained busy with Pokémon Champions and Tomodachi Life: Living the Dream helping to reinforce the Switch 2's growing software library. Yet beneath the releases, much of the conversation still revolved around the months ahead. Grand Theft Auto VI remained the industry's gravitational centre despite its absence, with players and publishers alike measuring the rest of the year against the looming arrival of Rockstar's blockbuster. April felt like a month spent crossing items off long-running wishlists while simultaneously looking towards a future that seemed to dominate every discussion.

items.title: Starfield reaches PS5 on April 7.
items.body: It was once one of Xbox's biggest exclusives, but no Bethesda's Starfield finally crosses the divide to the old enemy. It's been so very long, though, would the PlayStation kids actually care?
items.platform: PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, Nintendo Switch 2, and PC

items.title: Pokemon Champions launches on April 8.
items.body: The Pokémon spin-off bringing multiplayer battling to the masses - well, the masses that have a Switch 2 at any rate. It was a welcome arrival for a console that needs big games.

items.title: Diablo IV: Lord of Hatred arrives
items.body: The long-awaited second expansion for Diablo IV brings the conclusion of one of Sanctuary's longest-running battles. It's time to make your final stand against Mephisto.

items.title: Pragmata launches on April 17.
items.body: Capcom's new IP, featuring a young AI girl raised eyebrows but actually turned out to be pretty good and sold well as people tried to get Diana and Hugh back to Earth.
items.platform: PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, Nintendo Switch 2, and PC

items.title: Windrose finally sails the High Seas
items.body: A mighty pivot on genre at the last minute proved the correct chose as the pirate-'em-up sold an absolute ton, and proved to be hugely fun to play too. Those f$%k$ng boars though. Please

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: The biggest releases in a traditionally quiet month

items.title: Starfield
items.body: Starfield is the first new universe in 25 years from Bethesda Game Studios, the award-winning creators of The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim and Fallout 4.

items.title: Pokemon Champions
items.body: Pokémon Champions is a strategy video game developed by The Pokémon Works and published by Nintendo and The Pokémon Company for the Nintendo Switch, Android and iOS. It is a spin-off of the Pokémon video game series focused on multiplayer gameplay

items.title: Windrose
items.body: Embark on a PvE survival adventure in the Age of Piracy. Fight on land and sea, solo or with friends. Build, craft and explore vast open world filled with dark secrets. Master soulslite combat and take on challenging bosses, command your ship and plunder unspoken treasures!
items.signal: Yo ho ho and a bottle of rum

items.title: Hades II
items.body: Battle beyond the Underworld using dark sorcery to take on the Titan of Time in this bewitching sequel to the award-winning rogue-like dungeon crawler.

items.title: Mouse: P.I. For Hire
items.body: Join private investigator Jack Pepper on a guns blazing, jazz-fueled adventure in MOUSE: P.I. For Hire. MOUSE combines the charm of hand-drawn rubber hose animation inspired by the classic cartoons of the 1930’s with the adrenaline and action of an explosive first person shooter.
items.platform: PS5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch 2, Switch, PC

items.title: Tomodachi Life: Living the Dream
items.body: Tomodachi Life: Living the Dream is the life and social simulation game for the Nintendo Switch that puts you in charge of a bustling, quirky island filled with custom Mii characters. It builds heavily on the classic 3DS original, adding deep Mii customization, island-building tools, and the creative Pallet House.

items.title: Pragmata
items.platform: PC / PS5 / Xbox Series X|S / Switch 2
items.body: Pragmata is a unique, sci-fi action-adventure game from Capcom. Follow Hugh, a member of an ill-fated investigation team, and Diana, a young android, as they navigate a lunar facility taken over by rogue AI in search of a way to Earth.

items.title: Masters of Albion
items.body: A bold reimagining of the God Game genre from the creator who defined it. Shape a living world as a god - or step into it and experience it through your people. Build, guide and defend your town by day, then survive the night when everything is under threat. There is no single solution. Only yours.

items.title: Sudden Strike 5
items.body: Sudden Strike 5 delivers real-time tactics in epic WWII battles. A host of tactical options and more than 300 playable units open-up multiple approaches to reach your objectives in a 25 mission campaign on historical battlefields in Europe and North Africa.
items.signal: War? What is it good for?

items.title: Saros
items.body: Beneath the shadow of an ominous eclipse, Arjun Devraj (Rahul Kohli, star of The Haunting of Bly Manor and Midnight Mass) is a Soltari enforcer who will stop at nothing to pursue answers on the shape-shifting world of Carcosa, a planet filled with dark secrets and hostile inhabitants.

items.title: Heroes of Might & Magic: Olden Era
items.body: Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era is the official prequel hailing back to the origins of the genre-defining, critically acclaimed series of turn-based strategy games. Raise grand armies and wield devastating spells to overcome your foes in both solo and multiplayer.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, subscription and buying context around the month.

items.title: PS5 gets more expensive instead of cheaper
items.body: Sony’s global price rise took effect on April 2, with PS5 moving to $649.99 in the US, £569.99 in the UK, and €649.99 in Europe. PS5 Pro rose to $899.99 / £789.99 / €899.99. It’s a useful “generation upside down” moment: the longer the console cycle went on, the less affordable the hardware became.

items.title: ROG Xbox Ally drops into Steam Deck territory
items.body: The base ROG Xbox Ally with Ryzen Z2A dropped to $499.99 on Amazon US, undercutting its listed $599.99 price and putting it below the 512GB Steam Deck OLED’s $549 starting point. Nice exhibit angle: Windows handhelds were starting to fight on price, not just specs.

items.title: OnePlus handheld leak points to phone brands chasing Steam Deck
items.body: A leaked render suggested OnePlus could be preparing a dedicated Android gaming handheld with an 8-inch display, integrated grips, shoulder buttons, and a flagship MediaTek Dimensity chip. Mark this one as lower-certainty, but historically interesting because it shows the handheld boom pulling in smartphone makers too

items.title: Xbox Mode and Auto SR push PC handhelds closer to consoles
items.body: Microsoft rolled out Xbox Mode for Windows 11 PCs and brought Auto Super Resolution preview to Xbox Insiders on ROG Xbox Ally X for docked play. The big signal here is the console-ification of PC handhelds: controller-first UI, docked TV behaviour, library aggregation, and AI upscaling trying to hide the messiness of Windows gaming.

items.title: Xbox Helix keeps blurring the line between console and PC
items.body: Notebookcheck covered Project Helix as Microsoft’s reported next-gen Xbox direction, with the machine looking more like a fixed-spec gaming PC than a traditional closed console. Nice future-history angle: April 2026 was when “what even is an Xbox now?” became a hardware question, not just a brand question.

items.title: Switch 2 dominates April hardware estimates
items.body: VGChartz estimates put Switch 2 well ahead in the Americas for April 2026, with 332,796 units versus 152,845 for PS5 and 66,250 for Xbox Series X/S. I’d mark this as “estimated” rather than official, but it makes a strong data-card for the month.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: What was on the shelves in April

items.title: Edge
items.headline: Edge on the 2026 shelf
items.curatorNote: An in-depth look at Krafton's upcoming cyberpunk romp No Law adorned the cover of Edge in April.
items.body: What was on the shelves in April

items.title: PC Gamer UK
items.headline: Dawn of War makes the cover
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed placeholder cover for PC Gamer UK, 2026 January-May.
items.curatorNote: Warhammer 40k's Dawn of War 4 gets the cover treatment. Inside is coverage of Mewgenics, Solasta II and Megabonk
items.body: The PC-facing drawer for Marathon, Slay the Spire 2 early access, Forza Horizon 6, and the online calendar.

items.title: Retro Gamer
items.headline: Retro Gamer on the 2026 shelf
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed placeholder cover for Retro Gamer, 2026 January-May.
items.curatorNote: 40 years of Zelda was the reason to buy April's Retro Gamer, with a cover featuring images from all the games over four decades.
items.body: Modern games keep recycling older names, remakes and archives; the past is still on the same shelf.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players discovered, argued about and shared the month.

items.title: Xbox Social Clubs quietly disappear
items.body: Microsoft removed community-created Xbox Social Clubs in April, pushing players toward messages, Discord, party chat, and Looking for Group instead. Nice “end of an era” card: console makers once tried to own the whole social layer; by 2026, even Xbox was admitting most of that life had moved elsewhere.

items.title: Roblox introduces Kids and Select accounts
items.body: Roblox announced age-based account types, with tighter controls around game access, chat, parental approvals, and developer requirements. This is a very 2026 online-life moment: the metaverse as playground, but also paperwork, verification, and compliance.

items.title: Stop Destroying Videogames gets its EU Parliament hearing
items.body: The European Parliament held a public hearing on the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative, which argues publishers should not be able to make sold games unplayable when servers close. Great exhibit angle: players were no longer just complaining about shutdowns online — they were dragging the issue into lawmaking rooms.

items.title: PlayStation players panic over reported 30-day digital licence checks
items.body: Reports claimed some PS4 and PS5 digital purchases were showing a 30-day online check-in requirement, sparking comparisons with Xbox One’s old DRM disaster. I’d mark this one as “reported/controversial” rather than fully settled, but it absolutely fits the month’s theme: the internet connection becoming part of the receipt.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading from the player side of recent history.

items.title: Owning games felt weirdly conditional
items.body: Digital libraries, licence worries, server closures and the Stop Destroying Videogames campaign made permanence feel like the month’s quiet anxiety.

items.title: Handhelds became the new console war
items.body:
Handhelds became the new console war
Switch 2 was settling in, Windows handhelds were getting cheaper, and Xbox was trying to make PC gaming feel more like the sofa.

items.title: Prices went the wrong way
items.body: The PS5 price rise made the generation feel upside down: hardware was ageing, but the cost of entry was still climbing.

items.title: Everything lived behind a login
items.body: From Roblox’s new child accounts to Xbox retiring old social spaces, April 2026 felt like gaming was being reorganised around identity, access, and control.
